stem: What is the primary function of a Bookwalter retractor system in abdominal surgery?
options: To suction blood from the operative field
To provide stable, self-retaining retraction of the abdominal wall using interchangeable blades mounted on a ring
To cut through fascial layers
To clamp mesenteric vessels
rationale: The Bookwalter retractor is a self-retaining system with a post-mounted ring and interchangeable blades that provides stable, adjustable abdominal wall retraction for complex abdominal procedures.

stem: Which surgical position places the patient face down with the chest and hips elevated on rolls?
options: Prone position
Lateral decubitus position
Supine position
Lithotomy position
rationale: The prone position places the patient face down, with chest and pelvic rolls used to elevate the torso and allow free abdominal movement, commonly used for spinal and posterior procedures.

stem: During a gastrectomy, the surgeon requests a GIA stapler. What does this instrument accomplish?
options: It places two double rows of staples and cuts between them to divide and seal tissue simultaneously
It removes previously placed staples
It only places staples without cutting tissue
It sutures tissue using absorbable thread
rationale: The GIA (gastrointestinal anastomosis) stapler fires two double staggered rows of staples and simultaneously cuts between them, allowing division and sealing of tissue in one step.

stem: What is the purpose of performing a 'flash' or immediate-use steam sterilization (IUSS)?
options: To disinfect instruments between cases for routine use
To sterilize implantable devices for immediate use
To sterilize unwrapped instruments needed urgently when no other sterile instrument is available
To sterilize all instruments as the standard method
rationale: IUSS (formerly flash sterilization) is an emergency sterilization method for unwrapped instruments needed immediately when no alternative sterile instrument is available. It should not be used routinely.

stem: Which organism commonly causes gas gangrene in surgical wounds?
options: Streptococcus pyogenes
Staphylococcus epidermidis
Clostridium perfringens
Escherichia coli
rationale: Clostridium perfringens is the most common cause of gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis), producing toxins that cause tissue necrosis and gas formation in contaminated wounds.

stem: During a total knee replacement, the surgeon asks for the tibial alignment guide. What is its purpose?
options: To ensure accurate resection of the proximal tibia perpendicular to the mechanical axis
To measure the size of the tibial component
To drill pilot holes for the tibial implant
To apply bone cement to the tibial surface
rationale: The tibial alignment guide is used to position the cutting jig for accurate proximal tibial resection perpendicular to the mechanical axis, ensuring proper alignment of the tibial component.

stem: What is the difference between disinfection and sterilization?
options: Sterilization only kills bacteria while disinfection kills all microorganisms
Disinfection is more effective than sterilization
They are the same process with different names
Disinfection reduces microorganisms to a safe level while sterilization destroys all forms of microbial life including spores
rationale: Disinfection reduces pathogenic microorganisms to a level that is not harmful, while sterilization is the complete destruction of all forms of microbial life including bacterial endospores.

stem: A surgical technologist is assisting with a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The surgeon reports difficulty identifying the anatomy. What should the surgical technologist anticipate?
options: Removing all trocars and repositioning them
Abandoning the procedure entirely
Conversion to an open procedure, requiring preparation of an open instrument tray
Increasing the insufflation pressure to maximum
rationale: When anatomical identification is difficult during laparoscopic surgery, conversion to an open procedure may be necessary. The surgical technologist should anticipate this and have an open tray available.

stem: Which artery is commonly used for arterial blood gas (ABG) sampling and arterial line placement in the perioperative setting?
options: Dorsalis pedis artery
Femoral artery
Radial artery
Brachial artery
rationale: The radial artery is preferred for ABG sampling and arterial line placement due to its superficial location, accessibility, and the presence of collateral circulation from the ulnar artery via the palmar arches.

stem: What is the recommended sequence for surgical skin preparation?
options: Begin at the periphery and work toward the incision site
Clean to dirty: begin at the incision site and move outward in concentric circles, never returning to a previously prepped area
Begin at the dirtiest area and move toward the cleanest area
Apply prep solution uniformly across the entire area at once
rationale: Surgical skin prep follows the clean-to-dirty principle: starting at the incision site and moving outward in concentric circles, never returning inward to prevent recontamination of the prepared area.

stem: During a thyroidectomy, the surgeon requests a nerve integrity monitor (NIM). What is its function?
options: To measure thyroid hormone levels during surgery
To ensure adequate anesthesia depth
To monitor cardiac rhythm during the procedure
To monitor the recurrent laryngeal nerve to prevent injury during dissection
rationale: The nerve integrity monitor (NIM) is used during thyroid surgery to identify and monitor the recurrent laryngeal nerve in real-time, alerting the surgeon to potential nerve injury during dissection.

stem: What is the most appropriate suture for vascular anastomosis?
options: Nylon monofilament
Chromic gut suture
Non-absorbable monofilament polypropylene (Prolene)
Absorbable braided polyglactin (Vicryl)
rationale: Polypropylene (Prolene) is the preferred suture for vascular anastomosis because it is non-absorbable, monofilament (reducing thrombogenicity), and has minimal tissue reactivity.

stem: A patient develops urticaria, bronchospasm, and hypotension during surgery. What condition is most likely occurring?
options: Anaphylaxis
Malignant hyperthermia
Myocardial infarction
Pneumothorax
rationale: Urticaria, bronchospasm, and hypotension are classic signs of anaphylaxis, a severe systemic allergic reaction that can occur during surgery from medications, latex, or other allergens.

stem: What is the purpose of the 'time out' procedure performed immediately before surgical incision?
options: To confirm that the patient's insurance has been verified
To verify the correct patient, correct procedure, correct site, and team agreement to proceed
To allow the surgeon to rest before the procedure
To check all instruments are present on the back table
rationale: The surgical timeout is a final safety check performed immediately before incision to verify correct patient identity, procedure, surgical site, and obtain team consensus before proceeding.

stem: Which type of scissors is most appropriate for cutting delicate tissue near vital structures?
options: Mayo scissors
Bandage scissors
Wire-cutting scissors
Metzenbaum scissors
rationale: Metzenbaum scissors have long handles with short, delicate blades ideal for cutting and dissecting delicate tissue near vital structures while minimizing surrounding tissue damage.

stem: What is the recommended aeration time for ethylene oxide (EO) sterilized items?
options: No aeration is needed for EO sterilized items
1 hour at room temperature
24 hours in a sealed container
8 to 12 hours in a mechanical aerator at 50-60°C
rationale: EO sterilized items require mechanical aeration at 50-60°C for 8-12 hours to allow toxic EO residuals to dissipate from the materials, preventing patient and staff exposure.

stem: During a coronary artery bypass graft procedure, what is the function of the cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) machine?
options: To temporarily take over the functions of the heart and lungs during surgery
To warm the surgical instruments
To continuously monitor blood pressure
To deliver anesthesia to the patient
rationale: The CPB machine diverts blood away from the heart and lungs, oxygenates it, and returns it to the body, allowing the surgeon to operate on a still, bloodless heart.

stem: What is the most important factor in preventing surgical site infections?
options: Using the newest surgical instruments
Administering the most potent antibiotic available
Keeping the operating room at the coldest possible temperature
Strict adherence to aseptic technique throughout the perioperative period
rationale: Strict adherence to aseptic technique is the single most important factor in preventing surgical site infections, encompassing hand hygiene, sterile technique, and environmental controls.

stem: Which instrument is used to grasp the gallbladder during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy?
options: Laparoscopic grasping forceps (Johan or Maryland dissector)
Kocher clamp
Babcock clamp
Allis clamp
rationale: Laparoscopic grasping forceps such as Johan graspers or Maryland dissectors are used to grasp and manipulate the gallbladder during laparoscopic cholecystectomy through trocar ports.

stem: What is the correct response when a surgical team member arrives late and the case is already in progress?
options: The late member must perform a complete surgical scrub, gown and glove, and be assisted into the sterile field by an already-scrubbed team member
The late member should not join the case at all
The late member can skip the surgical scrub if the procedure is almost over
The late member can join the sterile field after a quick hand wash
rationale: Any team member joining a case in progress must perform a complete surgical scrub and be properly gowned and gloved before entering the sterile field, regardless of the procedure status.

stem: Which anatomical landmark is used to identify the appendix during an appendectomy?
options: The convergence of the three taeniae coli at the base of the cecum
The hepatic flexure of the colon
The ileocecal valve only
The sigmoid colon junction
rationale: The appendix is located at the convergence of the three taeniae coli (longitudinal muscle bands) at the base of the cecum, providing a reliable anatomical landmark during appendectomy.

stem: What is the purpose of using a wound protector during surgery?
options: To keep the wound open for visualization
To protect wound edges from contamination and tumor cell implantation during extraction
To deliver topical antibiotics to the wound
To apply pressure to bleeding wound edges
rationale: Wound protectors shield wound edges from contamination by gastrointestinal contents and prevent tumor cell implantation in the wound during specimen extraction in cancer surgery.

stem: What type of energy does an ultrasonic scalpel (Harmonic) use to cut and coagulate tissue?
options: Laser light energy
Mechanical vibration at 55,500 Hz to denature protein and seal vessels
Radiofrequency electrical energy
Thermal heat from a heating element
rationale: The Harmonic scalpel uses ultrasonic mechanical vibration at 55,500 Hz, which denatures protein in tissue and blood vessels to simultaneously cut and coagulate with minimal thermal spread.

stem: During a surgical procedure, the ESU dispersive pad becomes partially dislodged. What is the immediate action?
options: Tape the pad back in place and continue
Continue the procedure as long as the pad has some contact
Switch to bipolar electrosurgery without replacing the pad
Stop electrosurgery use immediately, notify the surgeon, and replace the dispersive pad before resuming
rationale: A partially dislodged dispersive pad concentrates the return current, increasing the risk of a burn at the pad site. Use must be stopped immediately and the pad replaced before resuming.

stem: Which type of hernia occurs through the internal (deep) inguinal ring and travels along the inguinal canal?
options: Umbilical hernia
Direct inguinal hernia
Indirect inguinal hernia
Femoral hernia
rationale: An indirect inguinal hernia passes through the internal (deep) inguinal ring and travels along the inguinal canal, potentially extending into the scrotum. It follows the path of testicular descent.

stem: What is the primary purpose of using a cottonoid (patty) in neurosurgery?
options: To stop major arterial bleeding in the brain
To protect delicate neural tissue and provide a moist barrier during retraction and dissection
To absorb excess irrigation fluid from the field
To pack the sinus cavity after sinus surgery
rationale: Cottonoids (patties) are small, radiopaque compressed cotton pledgets used in neurosurgery to protect delicate neural tissue during retraction and to provide a moist working surface.

stem: A patient develops deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the postoperative period. Which factor from Virchow's triad most directly contributed?
options: Venous stasis from immobility during and after surgery
Excessive surgical hand scrubbing
Ambient operating room temperature
Use of non-absorbable sutures
rationale: Virchow's triad (venous stasis, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability) describes DVT risk factors. Venous stasis from immobility during and after surgery is the most directly modifiable factor.

stem: What is the function of a Fogarty balloon catheter in vascular surgery?
options: To deliver stents to narrowed vessels
To remove thrombus or embolus from blood vessels by inflating the balloon beyond the clot and withdrawing it
To dilate narrowed coronary arteries
To measure blood flow through a vessel
rationale: The Fogarty balloon catheter is passed beyond the clot, the balloon is inflated, and the catheter is withdrawn, pulling the thrombus or embolus out of the vessel (embolectomy/thrombectomy).

stem: What is the minimum number of sponge counts required during a surgical procedure?
options: At least three: before the procedure, before closure begins, and at skin closure
Two counts: at the beginning and end only
Counts are optional and at the surgeon's discretion
One count at the end of the procedure
rationale: Standard practice requires a minimum of three counts: an initial count before the procedure, a count before closure of a body cavity or wound begins, and a final count at skin closure.

stem: Which disinfection level is required for semi-critical items such as respiratory equipment and endoscopes?
options: High-level disinfection
Intermediate-level disinfection
Low-level disinfection
Sterilization only
rationale: Semi-critical items that contact mucous membranes or non-intact skin require high-level disinfection, which destroys all microorganisms except high numbers of bacterial spores.

stem: During an inguinal hernia repair, the surgeon identifies the ilioinguinal nerve. Why is preservation of this nerve important?
options: To maintain bowel function
To prevent chronic groin pain and loss of sensation to the medial thigh and scrotum/labia
To prevent urinary retention
To maintain hip flexion ability
rationale: The ilioinguinal nerve provides sensory innervation to the medial thigh and genital area. Damage during hernia repair can cause chronic post-herniorrhaphy groin pain and numbness.

stem: What is the appropriate way to handle a tissue specimen obtained during surgery?
options: Hand it directly to the pathology technician
Pass it off the field immediately in a properly labeled specimen container with preservative if required
Keep it on the back table until the end of the case
Place it in a dry basin on the mayo stand
rationale: Tissue specimens should be passed off the sterile field promptly in a correctly labeled container with appropriate preservative (such as formalin) to ensure proper identification and tissue preservation.

stem: Which instrument is specifically designed for grasping bowel tissue atraumatically?
options: Allis tissue forceps
Ochsner clamp
Kocher clamp
Babcock forceps
rationale: Babcock forceps have broad, fenestrated, smooth (non-toothed) jaws designed to gently grasp bowel and other delicate tubular structures without causing tissue damage.

stem: What is the appropriate interval for checking tourniquet inflation time during orthopedic surgery?
options: Tourniquet time does not need to be monitored
Every 2 hours
Every 30 minutes, and the surgeon should be notified when the recommended maximum time is approaching
Only at the end of the procedure
rationale: Tourniquet time should be monitored every 30 minutes and the surgeon notified as the maximum recommended time (typically 1-2 hours) approaches to prevent ischemic complications.

stem: Which surgical procedure involves the creation of an opening between the stomach and jejunum?
options: Gastrostomy
Gastrojejunostomy
Pyloroplasty
Jejunostomy
rationale: Gastrojejunostomy creates an anastomosis between the stomach and the jejunum, bypassing the duodenum, often performed for gastric outlet obstruction or as part of a Billroth II procedure.

stem: What is the role of macrophages in the inflammatory phase of wound healing?
options: Phagocytosis of bacteria and debris, and release of growth factors that stimulate tissue repair
Production of collagen for wound strength
Production of scar tissue for wound closure
Formation of new blood vessels only
rationale: Macrophages are essential in wound healing as they phagocytose bacteria and cellular debris and release cytokines and growth factors that recruit fibroblasts and stimulate angiogenesis.

stem: During a spinal procedure, the surgical technologist is asked to prepare methylene blue dye. What is its surgical use?
options: To flush the IV line
To disinfect the surgical site
To identify cerebrospinal fluid leaks and fistula tracts
To stain tissue for pathology examination
rationale: Methylene blue dye is used intraoperatively to identify CSF leaks, fistula tracts, and sinus tracts by highlighting the pathway of fluid flow for precise surgical repair.

stem: What is the correct method for opening a sterile wrapped instrument set on a back table?
options: Open the fold closest to you first
Open all folds simultaneously by lifting the top layer
Have two people open opposite sides at the same time
Open the first fold away from you, open the side folds, then open the last fold toward you without reaching across the sterile field
rationale: The correct sequence prevents contamination: open the first fold away from you, open the side folds, and open the last fold toward you. Never reach across the sterile field.

stem: Which vessel is most commonly used as a conduit for coronary artery bypass grafting?
options: Left internal mammary artery (LIMA) and saphenous vein
External jugular vein
Cephalic vein
Inferior epigastric artery
rationale: The LIMA is the gold standard conduit for LAD grafting due to superior long-term patency. The saphenous vein is the most commonly used supplemental conduit for other coronary targets.

stem: What is the function of the esophageal stethoscope during general anesthesia?
options: To deliver oxygen to the patient
To measure blood pressure
To continuously monitor heart sounds and respiratory sounds from within the esophagus
To monitor brain activity
rationale: The esophageal stethoscope is placed in the esophagus near the heart to provide continuous monitoring of heart sounds and respiratory sounds during general anesthesia.

stem: What is the primary difference between monopolar and bipolar electrosurgery?
options: There is no functional difference
Monopolar is safer than bipolar
Bipolar requires a dispersive pad and monopolar does not
In monopolar, current passes through the patient to a dispersive pad; in bipolar, current passes only between the two tips of the instrument
rationale: In monopolar ESU, current flows from the active electrode through the patient to the dispersive pad. In bipolar, current flows only between the two tips of the forceps, limiting thermal spread.

stem: During a mastoidectomy, which instrument is used to remove bone from the mastoid process?
options: Mastoid drill with cutting and diamond burrs
Gigli saw
Oscillating saw
Kerrison rongeur
rationale: A mastoid drill with cutting burrs (for bone removal) and diamond burrs (for smoothing near vital structures) is used to remove bone during mastoidectomy for exposure of the mastoid air cells.

stem: What is the appropriate action if a needle is missing during the closing count?
options: Close the wound and do a postoperative X-ray
Assume the needle was discarded and continue
Continue closing and document the missing needle
Notify the surgeon immediately, search the field and wound, and obtain an intraoperative X-ray if the needle is not found
rationale: A missing needle is a critical safety issue. The surgeon must be immediately notified, a thorough search conducted, and intraoperative imaging obtained if the needle is not located before closure.

stem: Which fascial layer forms the floor of the inguinal canal?
options: Transversalis fascia
Scarpa's fascia
Camper's fascia
Lumbodorsal fascia
rationale: The transversalis fascia forms the posterior wall (floor) of the inguinal canal. Direct inguinal hernias protrude through a weakness in this fascia (Hesselbach's triangle).

stem: What is the purpose of using an Esmarch bandage before tourniquet inflation?
options: To apply pressure to a bleeding wound
To protect the skin from tourniquet pressure
To splint a fractured extremity
To exsanguinate the limb by wrapping from distal to proximal before tourniquet inflation
rationale: The Esmarch bandage is a wide elastic bandage wrapped tightly from distal to proximal to exsanguinate (drain blood from) the extremity before tourniquet inflation for a bloodless field.

stem: Which solution is most commonly used for irrigating the surgical field to remove debris and reduce bacterial contamination?
options: Sterile distilled water
Hydrogen peroxide 3%
Povidone-iodine solution full strength
Warm sterile normal saline (0.9% NaCl)
rationale: Warm sterile normal saline is isotonic and biocompatible, making it the standard irrigation solution for removing debris and diluting bacterial contamination during surgery.

stem: What is the function of a vessel loop in surgery?
options: To encircle and identify blood vessels, nerves, or other tubular structures for retraction and isolation
To suture vessels closed
To dilate narrowed vessels
To measure the diameter of blood vessels
rationale: Vessel loops are thin, flexible silicone strips used to encircle and identify blood vessels, nerves, ducts, or other tubular structures, allowing gentle retraction and isolation during dissection.

stem: During a laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon reports a cloudy image on the monitor. What should the surgical technologist do first?
options: Clean the laparoscope lens with anti-fog solution or warm saline and wipe with a lint-free cloth
Increase the light source intensity to maximum
Replace the entire camera system
Turn off the camera and restart the system
rationale: A cloudy laparoscopic image is most commonly caused by lens fogging from condensation. The first action is to clean the lens with anti-fog solution or warm saline and wipe it clean.

stem: Which type of wound closure is performed when a contaminated wound is left open initially and closed surgically 3-5 days later?
options: Spontaneous closure
Primary intention
Tertiary intention (delayed primary closure)
Secondary intention
rationale: Tertiary intention (delayed primary closure) involves leaving a contaminated wound open for 3-5 days for observation and infection control, then surgically closing it once it appears clean.

stem: What is the maximum recommended inflation pressure for a pneumatic tourniquet on the upper extremity?
options: 100 to 150 mmHg
50 to 75 mmHg
400 to 500 mmHg
250 to 300 mmHg
rationale: The recommended tourniquet pressure for the upper extremity is approximately 250-300 mmHg (typically 50-75 mmHg above systolic blood pressure) to achieve arterial occlusion without excessive pressure.

stem: A surgical technologist is scrubbed in for an exploratory laparotomy. The surgeon discovers a perforated bowel. How does this change the wound classification?
options: The classification remains unchanged
The wound is reclassified from Class II (Clean-Contaminated) to Class IV (Dirty/Infected)
It becomes Class I (Clean)
It becomes Class III (Contaminated) only
rationale: A perforated bowel with spillage of contents changes the wound classification to Class IV (Dirty/Infected) due to the presence of devitalized tissue and established infection.

stem: What is the function of the internal fixation plate in fracture repair?
options: To stimulate bone growth through electrical impulses
To compress soft tissue around the fracture
To bridge the fracture and maintain bone alignment during healing by distributing stress across the fracture site
To replace the fractured bone segment permanently
rationale: Internal fixation plates bridge the fracture, maintain alignment, and distribute mechanical stress across the fracture site, allowing bone healing while providing stability.

stem: What is the appropriate response when the sterile field becomes contaminated during a procedure?
options: Continue and document the contamination in the postoperative report
Spray the contaminated area with antiseptic and continue
Notify the surgeon, isolate the contaminated area, replace contaminated items, and regown/reglove if necessary
Ignore it if the contamination appears minor
rationale: Any contamination of the sterile field requires immediate action: notifying the surgeon, isolating the area, replacing contaminated items, and having affected team members regown and reglove.

stem: Which ligament connects the liver to the anterior abdominal wall and is encountered during upper abdominal surgery?
options: Inguinal ligament
Broad ligament
Falciform ligament
Cooper's ligament
rationale: The falciform ligament connects the liver to the anterior abdominal wall and diaphragm. It contains the ligamentum teres (remnant of the umbilical vein) and is encountered during upper abdominal surgery.

stem: What is the primary purpose of using a trocar with an optical viewing tip during laparoscopic port placement?
options: To provide a wider working channel for instruments
To allow visual identification of tissue layers during insertion, reducing the risk of organ injury
To automatically place the cannula at the correct depth
To eliminate the need for pneumoperitoneum
rationale: Optical viewing trocars allow the surgeon to see tissue layers as they are penetrated during insertion, significantly reducing the risk of inadvertent bowel or vascular injury during access.

stem: What personal protective equipment must be worn when handling glutaraldehyde solutions?
options: No special PPE is required
Only a face shield is needed
Standard surgical gloves only
Chemical-resistant gloves, eye protection, and a mask with organic vapor cartridge in a well-ventilated area
rationale: Glutaraldehyde is toxic and requires chemical-resistant gloves, eye protection, and respiratory protection (organic vapor cartridge mask) in a well-ventilated area to prevent exposure.

stem: During a hip fracture repair with a dynamic hip screw (DHS), what radiographic imaging is essential?
options: Intraoperative fluoroscopy (C-arm) to confirm proper screw placement and fracture reduction
No imaging is needed if the surgeon is experienced
Preoperative MRI only
Postoperative CT scan only
rationale: Intraoperative fluoroscopy (C-arm) is essential during DHS placement to confirm proper guide wire and screw position within the femoral head and verify fracture reduction in real-time.

stem: What is the primary characteristic of a keloid scar?
options: Excessive collagen deposition that extends beyond the original wound margins
A scar caused by wound infection
Normal scar tissue that remains within the wound margins
A scar that is indented below the skin surface
rationale: Keloids are characterized by excessive collagen deposition that extends beyond the original wound boundaries, distinguishing them from hypertrophic scars which remain within wound margins.

stem: Which retractor is commonly used for exposure during an open heart procedure through a median sternotomy?
options: Army-Navy retractor
Lone Star retractor
Sternal retractor (Finochietto or Ankeney)
Balfour retractor
rationale: A sternal retractor (such as Finochietto or Ankeney) is specifically designed to spread the sternum after median sternotomy, providing wide exposure of the mediastinal contents for cardiac surgery.

stem: What is the surgical technologist's role in the Universal Protocol for preventing wrong-site surgery?
options: The Universal Protocol is only the surgeon's responsibility
Only observe the timeout without participating
Verify the patient's identity but not the surgical site
Actively participate in the pre-procedure verification, site marking confirmation, and timeout process as a team member
rationale: The surgical technologist is an active participant in the Universal Protocol, including pre-procedure verification, confirming site marking, and participating in the timeout before incision.

stem: Which valve of the heart is most commonly replaced in cardiac surgery?
options: Pulmonic valve
Mitral valve
Tricuspid valve
Aortic valve
rationale: The aortic valve is the most commonly replaced cardiac valve due to the prevalence of aortic stenosis, particularly in the elderly population, and the significant impact on cardiac output.

stem: What is the purpose of using radiopaque sponges in surgery?
options: To allow detection by X-ray if a sponge is inadvertently retained in the patient
To increase the absorbency of the sponge
To make the sponges more visible on the surgical field
To prevent allergic reactions to the sponge material
rationale: Radiopaque sponges contain a detectable strip or marker that appears on X-ray, allowing detection of retained sponges that might otherwise be invisible during postoperative imaging.

stem: During a nephrectomy, the patient is placed in the lateral position with the kidney rest elevated. What complication is the surgical technologist most concerned about?
options: Hypothermia from cold IV fluids
Allergic reaction to the kidney rest material
Compression of the inferior vena cava and dependent lung ventilation-perfusion mismatch
Infection at the pressure points
rationale: In lateral position with an elevated kidney rest, compression of the IVC can reduce venous return and cardiac output, while the dependent lung may have ventilation-perfusion mismatch.

stem: What is the proper technique for counting sharps during a surgical procedure?
options: Only count suture needles at the end of the case
Count sharps by estimating the number in the sharps container
Count each sharp individually, point to it, and verify it is intact; both the scrub and circulator confirm the count simultaneously
The surgeon is responsible for counting sharps
rationale: Sharps counting requires individual verification of each sharp with visual confirmation of its integrity, performed simultaneously by the scrub technologist and circulating nurse.

stem: What type of laser is commonly used for retinal surgery?
options: Argon laser
CO2 laser
Excimer laser
Nd:YAG laser
rationale: The argon laser is commonly used in retinal surgery because its blue-green light is absorbed by hemoglobin and melanin, allowing precise photocoagulation of retinal tissue for conditions like retinal tears.

stem: Which phase of the cell cycle is targeted by most chemotherapy drugs and is relevant to understanding tumor biology in surgical oncology?
options: G1 phase (growth)
S phase (DNA synthesis)
M phase (mitosis)
G0 phase (resting)
rationale: Most chemotherapy drugs target the S phase (DNA synthesis) of the cell cycle, when cells are actively replicating their DNA. Understanding this helps surgical teams manage oncology patients.

stem: What is the appropriate action if sterile gloves become perforated during a procedure?
options: Remove the contaminated gloves immediately and replace with sterile gloves, changing the outer glove if double-gloved
Continue wearing them if the perforation is small
Apply a sterile adhesive strip over the perforation
Wait until the procedure is finished to change gloves
rationale: Perforated gloves compromise the sterile barrier and expose both the patient and the surgical team to contamination. They must be changed immediately upon discovery of the perforation.

stem: What is the role of platelets in primary hemostasis?
options: To adhere to damaged vessel walls and aggregate to form a temporary platelet plug
To dissolve existing blood clots
To produce fibrinogen for clot formation
To constrict blood vessels through chemical release only
rationale: In primary hemostasis, platelets adhere to exposed collagen in damaged vessel walls, become activated, and aggregate to form a temporary platelet plug, providing initial hemostasis.

stem: During a craniotomy for brain tumor removal, which instrument is used to carefully elevate the bone flap?
options: Penfield dissector or periosteal elevator
Retractor
Bone saw only
Osteotome and mallet
rationale: A Penfield dissector or periosteal elevator is used to gently separate the bone flap from the underlying dura mater after craniotomy cuts are completed, minimizing risk of dural tears.

stem: What is the main advantage of using a bipolar electrosurgical unit during neurosurgery?
options: Precise coagulation with minimal current spread to surrounding neural tissue
No need for any return electrode
Higher power output for large vessel coagulation
Faster cutting ability than monopolar
rationale: Bipolar electrosurgery is preferred in neurosurgery because the current passes only between the two forceps tips, providing precise coagulation with minimal thermal spread to adjacent neural tissue.

stem: What is the function of a chest tube placed after a thoracotomy?
options: To deliver medications directly into the lung
To provide supplemental oxygen to the patient
To monitor heart rhythm
To evacuate air and fluid from the pleural space and re-expand the lung
rationale: A chest tube is placed in the pleural space after thoracotomy to evacuate air (pneumothorax), blood (hemothorax), and fluid, allowing the lung to re-expand and restore negative pleural pressure.

stem: Which chemical indicator class provides the most reliable information about sterilization conditions achieved inside a package?
options: Class 1 process indicator (external tape)
Class 2 Bowie-Dick test indicator
Class 3 single-variable indicator
Class 5 integrating indicator
rationale: Class 5 integrating indicators respond to all critical sterilization parameters (time, temperature, and steam) and provide the most reliable internal verification of sterilization conditions.

stem: What is the correct sequence for removing a contaminated gown and gloves after a procedure?
options: Remove the mask first, then gown, then gloves
Remove gloves first, then remove the gown
Remove the gown first by pulling it forward off the shoulders, then remove gloves using the glove-to-glove and skin-to-skin technique
Remove the gown and gloves simultaneously
rationale: The gown is removed first by pulling it forward off the shoulders (it is considered contaminated on the front), then gloves are removed using glove-to-glove and skin-to-skin technique.

stem: During an endoscopic sinus surgery, which landmark is critical for the surgeon to identify to prevent intracranial entry?
options: Cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone
Sphenoid sinus
Inferior turbinate
Maxillary sinus floor
rationale: The cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone forms the roof of the nasal cavity and is very thin. Penetration during sinus surgery can result in CSF leak and potential intracranial complications.

stem: What is the primary benefit of using antimicrobial-impregnated sutures in surgical wound closure?
options: Reduction of bacterial colonization along the suture line, decreasing surgical site infection risk
Improved cosmetic appearance of the wound
Faster suture absorption time
Increased tensile strength of the suture
rationale: Antimicrobial-impregnated sutures (such as Vicryl Plus with triclosan) reduce bacterial colonization along the suture tract, which has been shown to decrease surgical site infection rates.

stem: Which type of anesthesia is achieved by injecting local anesthetic around a specific nerve trunk?
options: General anesthesia
Peripheral nerve block
Topical anesthesia
Spinal anesthesia
rationale: A peripheral nerve block involves injecting local anesthetic around a specific nerve or nerve plexus to achieve regional anesthesia in the area innervated by that nerve.

stem: During a total hip arthroplasty, the surgeon requests a femoral broach. What is the purpose of this instrument?
options: To prepare the femoral canal to accept the femoral stem prosthesis by progressively enlarging the canal
To cut the femoral neck for head removal
To cement the prosthesis into position
To test the range of motion after implant placement
rationale: Femoral broaches are sequentially sized instruments used to progressively enlarge and shape the femoral medullary canal to precisely match the femoral stem prosthesis for a press-fit or cemented implant.

stem: What is the typical absorption time for polyglactin 910 (Vicryl) suture?
options: 7 to 10 days
120 to 180 days
It is non-absorbable
56 to 70 days by hydrolysis
rationale: Polyglactin 910 (Vicryl) is absorbed by hydrolysis in approximately 56-70 days, with approximately 75% of tensile strength lost by 2 weeks and complete absorption by 56-70 days.

stem: Which position places the patient supine with the head lower than the feet?
options: Reverse Trendelenburg position
Fowler's position
Jackknife position
Trendelenburg position
rationale: Trendelenburg position places the patient supine with the head lower than the feet, commonly used for lower abdominal and pelvic procedures to shift abdominal contents cephalad.

stem: What is the primary purpose of using a surgical smoke evacuator during electrosurgery and laser procedures?
options: To reduce the noise from electrosurgical units
To cool the surgical site during cauterization
To remove surgical plume containing potentially harmful chemicals, viable cellular material, and viral particles
To improve the surgeon's view by removing steam
rationale: Surgical smoke evacuators remove plume generated by electrosurgery and laser procedures, which contains toxic chemicals, viable bacteria, blood fragments, and potentially viable viral DNA.

stem: A surgical technologist is setting up for a sentinel lymph node biopsy. What radioactive tracer or dye is commonly used?
options: Technetium-99m sulfur colloid and/or isosulfan blue (Lymphazurin)
No tracer is needed for sentinel node biopsy
Methylene blue only
Indocyanine green only
rationale: Sentinel lymph node biopsy commonly uses technetium-99m sulfur colloid for radioactive detection and/or isosulfan blue dye for visual identification of the first draining lymph node.

stem: What is the purpose of performing the Allen test before radial artery cannulation?
options: To check for peripheral neuropathy
To test the patient's grip strength
To measure blood pressure in the arm
To verify adequate collateral circulation from the ulnar artery before radial artery access
rationale: The Allen test verifies adequate collateral circulation through the ulnar artery and palmar arches before radial artery cannulation, ensuring hand perfusion will be maintained.

stem: What type of bacteria forms endospores that are highly resistant to sterilization?
options: Bacillus and Clostridium species
Escherichia coli
Staphylococcus species
Streptococcus species
rationale: Bacillus and Clostridium are gram-positive, spore-forming genera that produce highly resistant endospores capable of surviving environmental extremes, making them the standard challenge for sterilization.

stem: During a hip arthroplasty, the patient suddenly becomes hypotensive and hypoxic during cement insertion. What complication should be suspected?
options: Pneumothorax
Malignant hyperthermia
Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS)
Allergic reaction to surgical drapes
rationale: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) occurs when pressurized methylmethacrylate cement forces fat, marrow, and air emboli into the bloodstream, causing acute hypotension, hypoxia, and cardiac arrest.

stem: Which instrument is used to apply hemostatic clips during laparoscopic surgery?
options: Laparoscopic scissors
Veress needle
Laparoscopic clip applier
Harmonic scalpel
rationale: The laparoscopic clip applier is used to apply titanium or polymer hemostatic clips to blood vessels and ductal structures during laparoscopic procedures for occlusion before division.

stem: What is the primary risk of Trendelenburg position during surgery?
options: Increased bleeding at the surgical site
Cervical spine injury
Increased intracranial pressure and respiratory compromise from cephalad shift of abdominal contents
Lower extremity ischemia
rationale: Trendelenburg position causes abdominal viscera to shift cephalad, compressing the diaphragm and reducing lung compliance, while also increasing intracranial and intraocular pressure.

stem: What is the correct procedure for handling a dropped but uncontaminated instrument that falls within the OR but remains on a clean surface?
options: If it landed on a clean floor, it can be resterilized quickly and returned
The instrument is considered contaminated regardless and must not be used on the sterile field
Wipe it with alcohol and return it to use
Pick it up and return it to the back table
rationale: Any instrument that leaves the sterile field is considered contaminated regardless of the surface it contacts. It must be removed from the sterile field and replaced with a sterile instrument.

stem: Which structure connects the testes to the urethra and is encountered during inguinal hernia surgery?
options: Seminal vesicle
Urethra
Ureter
Vas deferens (ductus deferens)
rationale: The vas deferens (ductus deferens) is a component of the spermatic cord that travels through the inguinal canal and must be identified and preserved during inguinal hernia repair.

stem: During a procedure, the surgeon requests Gelfoam. What is the mechanism of action of this hemostatic agent?
options: It chemically cauterizes bleeding vessels
It absorbs blood and provides a matrix for clot formation by activating the intrinsic coagulation pathway
It directly binds to and activates thrombin
It constricts blood vessels through vasoactive compounds
rationale: Gelfoam is an absorbable gelatin sponge that absorbs up to 45 times its weight in blood and provides a physical matrix that promotes platelet aggregation and activates the intrinsic coagulation pathway.

stem: What is the recommended humidity range for the operating room?
options: 70% to 90% relative humidity
Humidity does not affect the operating room environment
10% to 20% relative humidity
30% to 60% relative humidity
rationale: The recommended OR humidity is 30-60% to minimize static electricity (which increases fire and equipment risk at low humidity) and reduce microbial growth (which increases at high humidity).

stem: Which clamp is designed specifically for occluding the appendiceal stump before ligation?
options: Bulldog clamp
Satinsky clamp
Doyen clamp
Babcock clamp
rationale: While various clamps may be used, the Babcock clamp is commonly used to atraumatically grasp and occlude the appendiceal stump before ligation during appendectomy.

stem: What is the primary purpose of maintaining positive pressure ventilation in the operating room?
options: To ensure adequate oxygen supply for the surgical team
To prevent contaminated air from adjacent areas from flowing into the OR
To reduce noise from outside the OR
To keep the room temperature cool
rationale: Positive pressure ventilation ensures that air flows out of the OR when doors are opened, preventing potentially contaminated air from corridors and adjacent areas from entering the clean environment.

stem: During a colostomy creation, at which layer of the abdominal wall is the stoma typically matured?
options: At the fascial level only
At the peritoneal level
At the skin level, with the bowel everted and sutured to the dermis
At the subcutaneous fat layer
rationale: A stoma is matured by everting the bowel and suturing it to the dermis at the skin level, creating a protruding bud of bowel mucosa that facilitates appliance attachment and prevents retraction.

stem: What is the role of fibrin sealant (Tisseel) in surgery?
options: To dissolve blood clots in vessels
To replace sutures for all wound closures
To seal tissue surfaces and promote hemostasis by mimicking the final stages of the coagulation cascade
To disinfect the surgical site
rationale: Fibrin sealant combines fibrinogen and thrombin, mimicking the final stages of coagulation to form a fibrin clot that seals tissue surfaces, promotes hemostasis, and facilitates wound healing.

stem: What is the Spaulding classification system used to determine?
options: The infection risk category of surgical procedures
The sterility of the operating room air
The classification of surgical wounds
The level of disinfection or sterilization required based on the intended use of the medical device
rationale: The Spaulding classification categorizes medical devices as critical, semi-critical, or noncritical based on their intended use and degree of patient contact, determining the processing level required.

stem: During a repair of an abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA), what graft material is most commonly used?
options: Autologous saphenous vein graft
Bovine pericardial graft
ePTFE (Gore-Tex) graft
Woven or knitted Dacron (polyester) graft
rationale: Dacron (polyester) grafts, either woven or knitted, are the most commonly used synthetic grafts for AAA repair due to their strength, durability, and favorable tissue incorporation.

stem: What is the significance of the 'red line' on a gas cylinder in the operating room?
options: It indicates the gas is toxic
It marks the date of the last inspection
It shows the maximum safe pressure level
It indicates the cylinder is nearly empty and should be replaced soon
rationale: A red line or zone on a gas cylinder gauge indicates that the gas level is low and the cylinder should be replaced soon to avoid running out during a procedure.

stem: Which technique is used to reduce the bacterial load on skin before surgical incision?
options: Application of antiseptic surgical skin prep in a circular motion from the incision site outward
Applying a topical antibiotic ointment to the skin
Scrubbing the area with regular soap and water immediately before draping
Shaving the area with a razor 24 hours before surgery
rationale: Antiseptic surgical skin preparation applied from clean to dirty (incision site outward) reduces the transient and resident microbial flora on the skin, decreasing surgical site infection risk.

stem: Which condition is characterized by the presence of gas bubbles in tissue, crepitus, and a sweet or foul odor from a surgical wound?
options: Normal wound granulation
Seroma formation
Cellulitis
Gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis)
rationale: Gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis) is characterized by gas production in tissue (causing crepitus), tissue necrosis, and a characteristic sweet or foul odor, requiring emergent surgical debridement.

stem: What is the purpose of using a Doppler ultrasound probe during vascular surgery?
options: To measure tissue temperature
To guide needle placement for biopsies
To visualize bone fractures
To assess blood flow velocity and patency of blood vessels intraoperatively
rationale: A Doppler ultrasound probe is used intraoperatively during vascular surgery to assess blood flow velocity and confirm vessel patency before and after vascular reconstruction.
